data.title: Dividends By The Numbers In March 2025 And Q1 2025
data.description:
U.S. stock market dividends turned in a mostly negative performance in March 2025.

On the positive side of the ledger, March 2025's 16 dividend decreases came in below the 23 recorded in February 2025. Meanwhile, the year-over-year change in dividend reductions was little changed, increasing by just one over March 2024's total.

However, the change in the number of favorable changes like dividend increases, extra dividends and resumed dividends was decidedly negative. Combined, these three categories added to 141 favorable dividend actions for March 2025, which is 59 less than the 200 reported a year earlier.

This significant year-over-year reduction in favorable dividend actions combines with the small increase in unfavorable dividend changes to give March 2025 an overall score of -60. The following table presents Standard & Poor's dividend metadata for March 2025. It summarizes how the month's dividend data compares in both Month-over-Month (MoM) and Year-Over-Year (YoY) terms with previously reported data:

Dividend Changes in March 2025
Mar-2025 Feb-2025 MoM Mar-2024 YoY
Total Declarations 3,585 4,185 -600 ▼ 5,306 -1,721 ▼
Favorable 141 387 -246 ▼ 200 -59 ▼
- Increases 93 286 -193 ▼ 130 -37 ▼
- Special/Extra 47 99 -52 ▼ 67 -20 ▼
- Resumed 1 2 -1 ▼ 3 -2 ▼
Unfavorable 16 23 -7 ▼ 15 1 ▲
- Decreases 16 23 -7 ▼ 15 1 ▲
- Omitted/Passed 0 0 0 ◀▶ 0 0 ◀▶

The following chart shows how these numbers fit in the longer-running context of dividend increases and decreases since January 2004.

March 2025 represents a continuation of an ongoing negative trend for dividend increases that's been going on since the first quarter of 2023.

Switching to quarterly dividend results, the next chart groups the previous 15 months worth of dividend increase and decrease data into quarters, covering the last five quarters from 2024-Q1 through 2025-Q1. The chart shows 2025-Q1 was more negative for dividend-paying companies in the U.S. stock market than 2024-Q1 was.

The silver lining in this data is that the overall number of monthly dividend decreases remains below the threshold that is consistent with recessionary conditions being present in the U.S. economy.

But with a falling number of favorable dividend actions, how long might that continue?

References

Standard & Poor's. S&P Market Attributes Web File. [Excel Spreadsheet]. Accessed April 1, 2025.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Micron: If You're Questioning Gross Margins, I Have Your Answer
data.description:
As we headed into Micron's (NASDAQ:MU) FQ3 '25 earnings report the other week, I said there shouldn't be any surprises. And that was mainly the case. High bandwidth memory (HBM) is up with yields a bit better than expected. NAND continues to be in a downcycle, and gross margins are hovering around the mid-30s due to mix and different internal levers to navigate certain end market situations. There really wasn't too much there.

However, what has mounted now, after a guide for gross margins to be down incrementally quarter-over-quarter (again), is confusion among investors and observers as they see the lack of gross margin growth as HBM continues to dominate the headlines and grow massively.

But it's the last part people are taking for granted.

It's correct to believe if it weren't for HBM, DRAM would join its memory brethren NAND in the downcycle camp. After all, everything but the Data Center has been lackluster this past quarter and into this one, and even then only strong in the HBM and LPDRAM products for servers, specifically.

People are starting to question management on why this is the case.

"If HBM is doing so well, why isn't the company doing better?"

There are a few reasons, including the relative size of HBM for the company and how non-HBM products are faring in terms of demand (and supply with NAND).

To put this into perspective, HBM accounted for "over $1B" in revenue during the quarter. Let's call it $1.2B to be conservative and provide the benefit of the doubt. Likewise, high-capacity DRAM and LPDRAM for servers also reached $1B in revenue during the quarter. These are both considered high margin but not as high as HBM. Let's call this $1.1B.

The combination of our revenue from high-capacity DRAM modules and our industry-leading LPDRAM for the data center also exceeded the $1 billion milestone for the quarter.

- Sanajy, Mehrotra, CEO, Micron's FQ2 '25 Earnings Call

Therefore, only $2.3B of the quarter's $8.1B, or 28% were these high-margin, high-demand products. This means it made up almost 38% of DRAM revenue in the quarter. As you can see, 62% of DRAM revenue and 72% of overall revenue do not see this level of demand and margins.

To me, this isn't confusing as to why gross margins are only moderating in the 30s.

But let's dig deeper to understand how DRAM and NAND are doing otherwise and compare it to Micron's performance overall.

Digging Into The Two Memory Types

As we know, NAND is in a downcycle as the company continues to underutilize WFE and has begun to structurally reduce output by mimicking the same strategy it used in DRAM last fiscal year to slow bit growth but still move to leading-edge nodes to get the cost downs. However, DRAM is also in a modest (but not extreme) downcycle in mobile, consumer, automotive, and industrial. These sectors were down quarter-over-quarter and, in most cases, year-over-year.

Now, you have to remember, NAND is mixed into these business units except for Compute in varying degrees. This is why the Compute (and Networking) unit is not seeing much of a hit, though it includes PC and graphics DRAM, which has slowed a bit, but not to the extent NAND, particularly in the Data Center, has, which was down 20% quarter-over-quarter, though up 54% year-over-year.

As you can see, there's no direct data on where NAND is taking a hit vs. DRAM relative to the specific sector. The only pure-play business unit is Compute and Networking, and it only tells us four DRAM markets (data center, PC, graphics, and networking). This doesn't include mobile, auto, industrial, and consumer embedded DRAM, which is in Mobile and Embedded.

But, it's enough to understand everything except for Compute and Networking was down quarter over quarter, with Mobile and Storage the biggest losers. So, with the idea demand isn't chipper across the board, let's get back to the gross margin point.

Digging Into Their Respective Gross Margins

While Compute was up, it doesn't mean data center was all of it. Going forward, consumer PC and graphics DRAM growth is a headwind to the massive margins HBM contributes (some estimate 70% GMs) since consumer DRAM (and NAND) are of lower margins. This is called mix. I'll get to why this is important in a moment.

To come back to a statement I made earlier in this article, if we back out the HBM and high-margin products, DRAM would also look like a moderate cycle correction right now. This is important to keep in mind while considering the bigger picture.

But here's what's happening. NAND is hurting, and outside of HBM and high-margin LPDRAM for AI servers, DRAM is treading water in terms of bits shipped with indications of it slowly returning to growth in average selling prices (ASP). The cycles in the two memory types are somewhat misaligned in 2025, where generally they are in synchronous declining sine waves of sorts (it's not usually gradual up and gradual down, but you get the point).

So if NAND prices and bits shipped aren't up when DRAM prices and bits shipped are up, there's not a nice tracking of metrics, including margins, to become a clean sequential upcycle like we're used to seeing. Then, you add in HBM which has thrown a wrench into this as it "makes up for" other weaker DRAM products in the consumer, mobile, and auto businesses, and margins aren't so easy to read.

But there's more to it then just demand.

The next question is, have DRAM and NAND prices actually recovered from prior peaks?

Obviously, there isn't an expectation for the same dollar prices since technology costs come down over time, related to Wright's Law, and separately but similarly, Moore's Law. Moreover, memory isn't so cut and dry. New technologies aren't like transistors in a CPU, which get smaller and smaller but function largely the same way. DRAM technologies get more complicated. DDR4 to DDR5 is an example, as it has two new logic components that DDR4 doesn't have. This adds to the costs of DDR5; thus, the gross margin profile won't be the same as the later generations of DDR4. However, the price of DDR5 is higher than DDR4. In the end, you can see there's this give-and-take on both sides of the equation.

But, above all, we haven't seen the surge in DRAM pricing like in years past. It's been more subdued in terms of the strength of pricing. To illustrate this, I took a starting point of $8.00 in ASPs (the dollar amount is really for presentation purposes) and followed Micron's earnings reports using their percentage of quarter-over-quarter changes each quarter. Similarly, I took Micron's FQ2 '18 reported DRAM gross margin of 66% (yes, it was still providing DRAM versus NAND gross margins then), giving me its average cost of DRAM, and then followed its guidance (and confirmations) for cost-downs over the years. We wind up in FQ2 '25 with a gross margin for DRAM of about 54% (excluding HBM). This is what it looks like:

Now, obviously, that's not the complete picture, so I ran the same exercise for NAND. Things are much tighter in NAND. In 2018, the company's gross margins were 47% for NAND. However, NAND very quickly came down in price from 2018 to 2020, steeper than DRAM. Over the last several years, NAND has had negative gross margins several times, while DRAM barely had it occur once.

With this in mind, we can build a gross margin profile to explain why, even with HBM and the spending for all things AI, it doesn't matter if the upturn in DRAM is happening or HBM is growing wild percentages, the downturn in NAND with its typical razor-thin margins is keeping overall company margins compressed.

Using the assumption HBM revenue of $1.2B tracks at 70% gross margins, I'll place the high-capacity server and LPDRAM at 61%. And through my pricing exercise, the remaining DRAM comes to about 54% (though for DRAM and NAND in 2025, I factored in the full-year cost downs already, so this calculation will back out half of that), giving us an average gross margin for DRAM of 59% in FQ2.

For NAND, it's currently looking at a -20% gross margin, and on its $1.9B in revenue, it lost $380M in FQ2. With that in mind, the total company gross margin calculates to 39%, just slightly higher than the company's reported 38%. That's not bad for a back-of-the-envelope tracking of pricing and cost-downs from 2018 to now! It means I'm pretty close. I may be off on individual categories of products, and I'm willing to fine-tune them, but overall, the bigger picture is clear.

So, this exercise says the bottom line is NAND has more sway on the company than you might have first thought, while it's currently more of a drag on the company than first expected. But not all is to blame on NAND. Even with HBM, DRAM margins are still down seven percentage points from those 2018 highs. Pricing matters, and right now, the price of DRAM is not at the level we've seen in other upcycles compared to the costs. Thus, combined with NAND in a downcycle, this upcycle seems (and is) muted.

All Is Not Lost Moving Forward

But I'd be remiss if I didn't give you the picture before us.

If NAND is in the dumps now, but the cost downs for this year are some of the highest the company has seen historically, then when NAND demand recovers, and the producers get supply under control, Micron will have a more structurally sound cost basis for when ASPs find an upward trend.

We expect fiscal 2025 NAND front-end cost reductions to be in the low-teens percentage range.

- Sanajy, Mehrotra, CEO, Micron's FQ1 '25 Earnings Call

In other words, when NAND recovers, and DRAM simply continues on this slow recovery path, margins could quickly get into the 40s. For example, if NAND margins were breakeven this past quarter, the same revenue would have provided a 44.5% gross margin. A 10% NAND gross margin would push the same quarter to 46.7% overall.

The question now is, what does the rest of the fiscal and calendar year look like in terms of NAND and DRAM pricing, cost downs, and demand (bits shipped)? I've already tired you out in this article, so let's save it for the next one. After all, this understanding was needed first to know how the outlook truly affects the financials.

Chart Analysis

In addition to this confusion over gross margins, the stock price appears to reflect the uncertainty surrounding this upcycle. As I've discussed for the last several months, Micron is in a very messy consolidation, and after the last few weeks, it may be more complex than I initially had in mind. Nevertheless, I now understand better what it's trying to do.

Micron has been working through sideways consolidation for the better part of eight months. More recently, it has been working in a triangle pattern, but it's nearing the end of that pattern and should break out to the upside once complete, at least in the short term. It has a shot to head to the (B) wave target shown above between $115 and $120. Once it reaches the (B) wave target, wave (C) should start to the downside, targeting the $70s. All in all, there should be one more move higher (the wave [C] of (B)) to exit long positions before we move into the final stage of this correction (or at least this leg of it).

Overall, Micron is in a messy situation, both in the memory cycle and the chart. For now, I'm not going to make any serious moves with my long position, though, I may trim again on a move to $115-plus. For the time being, both the memory market and the chart need to work through some baggage before we can see Micron settle into some type of advancement.

data.title: ASML: How Secure Is Its EUV Monopoly?
data.description:
ASML (NASDAQ:ASML) is the 800-pound gorilla in the lithography market due to being the only one able to produce cutting-edge EUV (Extreme Ultraviolet) lithography machines able to produce the most advanced chips with the most miniature circuitry (3nm).

The company looks set to be a main beneficiary of the AI revolution, while training huge LLMs is running into diminishing returns, the market is shifting towards inference and reasoning models.

The inference market is likely to be multiples in size and reasoning models can take up to 100x the processing power compared to their predecessors, so the demand for AI chips is likely to remain very strong in the coming years as this is still in the very early innings (Q4 conference call, our emphasis):

Looking to 2025, we see full year revenue between €30 billion and €35 billion and gross margin between 51% and 53%. Consistent with our view from last quarter, artificial intelligence has become the key driver for growth in our industry at this moment. As we have witnessed in 2024, AI has created a shift in the market dynamics that is not benefiting all customers equally in the short term. If AI demand continues to be strong and customers are successful in bringing on additional capacity online to support that demand, there is potential opportunity towards the upper end of our range. On the other hand, there are also risks related to customers and geopolitics that could drive results towards the lower end of the range.

Other segments also see an upturn (Q4CC):

Looking at market segments, we currently expect Logic to be up versus 2024 with the ramp of leading-edge nodes while we expect Memory to remain strong, similar to 2024. With respect to our Installed Base business, we expect revenue to grow versus 2024 driven by both service and upgrades as part of a growing install base, to which EUV's contribution is continuing to grow.

New products

From the Q4 conference call (Q4CC):

In 2024 we have also successfully achieved a number of technology milestones, including the release of a number of new products critical for our customer’s technology roadmaps. On our Low NA EUV technology, the NXE:3800E, we demonstrated the full system specification in our factory with 220 wafers per hour throughput at a new record overlay. We are on track to deliver a new systems at full specification and start upgrades for the system already at our customers during the first half of 2025. We continue to work with our customers to drive the maturation of the system to support their ramp to high volume manufacturing.

The company delivered the first NXT:2150i immersion system and saw a 20% increase in metrology and inspection system sales, driven by YieldStar and multibeam inspection systems.

Finances

After a pretty rough year, the company is back on track:

This was somewhat surprising as the company's China business actually boomed during that period (Q4CC):

I think to be honest our view on China hasn't really changed in comparison to when we last spoke. Right. So when we last spoke we said the key dynamic on China is that and why China was so very strong in 2023 and 2024 now because for a number of years we have been building up a very significant backlog because all the fill rate for China was so low. That is the reason why the China sales in 2023 and 2024 was so high.

Revenue (+22.5% to E9.3B) came in above the high end of guidance mainly because of installed base revenue and the recognition of the sales of two High-NA systems.

Net bookings came in very strong, up 169% sequentially to E7.09B and way above analyst expectations (E3.99B). Management is discontinuing quarterly bookings in FY25 as these are too lumpy (Q4CC):

Q4 net system bookings came in at €7.1 billion which is made up of €3 billion of EUV and €4.1 billion of non EUV. Net system bookings in the quarter were driven by Logic with 61% of the bookings with Memory accounting for the remaining 39%.

Backlog is also substantial at E36B.

The operating margin has recovered nicely and so did cash flow, with a bumper Q4 with free cash flow at E8.84B, almost all the free cash flow for the year:

There hasn't been a lot of operating leverage in the model but if growth keeps at present levels that will produce some operating leverage.

Emerging competitive threats?

ASML does have legacy competition from Japan, but these haven't been able to keep up. Nikon and Canon offer complementary equipment, they primarily focus on legacy technologies like KrF (Krypton Fluoride) and I-line lithography systems that cannot compete at the cutting edge where EUV reigns.

However, Canon has spent 15 years developing a potential EUV alternative; Nanoimprint Lithography, which:

In contrast to conventional photolithography equipment, which transfers a circuit pattern by projecting it onto the resist coated wafer, the new product does it by pressing a mask imprinted with the circuit pattern on the resist on the wafer like a stamp. Because its circuit pattern transfer process does not go through an optical mechanism, fine circuit patterns on the mask can be faithfully reproduced on the wafer. Thus, complex two- or three-dimensional circuit patterns can be formed in a single imprint1, which may reduce the cost of ownership (CoO). Canon's NIL technology enables patterning with a minimum linewidth of 14 nm2, equivalent to the 5-nm-node3 required to produce most advanced logic semiconductors which are currently available. Furthermore, with further improvement of mask technology, NIL is expected to enable circuit patterning with a minimum linewidth of 10 nm, which corresponds to 2-nm-node.

The company already sold a machine to the Texas Institute for Electronics in 2024, a semiconductor consortium that includes AMD, Applied Materials, Intel, and Micron, but so far, demand isn't exploding despite the scarcity of cutting-edge lithography machines.

The sale was for a price that CEO Fujio Mitarai said would be "one digit less than ASML’s EUVs" (as well as using 90% less energy compared to ASML's EUV systems) which would enable smaller chip manufacturers to use advanced machines producing chips with smaller circuitry.

There are problems though:

because NIL is incompatible with both DUV and EUV, its insertion into current design flows is complicated, to say the least. Meanwhile, it is unclear whether it is possible to develop a fabrication process that can rely solely on nanoimprint lithography.

For now, this seems more of a niche solution, but there are improvements possible that could give it a wider appeal.

Chinese efforts

The US-imposed export restrictions which apply to ASML as well are not without risks:

  • The obvious risk is lost sales for Western companies in China, which is a large market for many of the cutting-edge chip equipment technology producers, ASML isn't an exception (they can still sell the older DUV machines).
  • It greatly incentivizes the Chinese to develop alternatives, and they are putting a lot of resources into this.

Sales to China are being affected as the backlog is tapering off, from the Q4CC:

Our China business in 2023 and 2024 was relatively high because of our ability to execute on a backlog that was created after low order fill rates in previous years. For 2025 and beyond, we expect our China business to go back to a more normalized percentage of our sales.

This demand won't go away as China (or more precisely SMIC) can produce chips with 7nm (and possibly stretch that to 5nm) circuitry and there are enough segments that don't need cutting-edge 2nm or 3nm based chips, but it's clear that selling EUV machines to China would be a large revenue generator for ASML if it was allowed.

The second risk, China developing home-grown alternatives to EUV begins to come into focus, here are some emerging solutions:

  • LDP (Huawei and SMEE)
  • Discharge plasma extreme ultraviolet lithography light source (Harbin Institute of Technology)
  • SAQP or Self-Aligned Quadruple Patterning (Huawei)
  • SiCarrier

To the surprise of many, China has already managed to produce chips with 7nm circuitry, like Huawei's Kerin 9010 produced by SMIC. These chips were produced by extending the capabilities of DUV machines, the type of lithography machines ASML can still sell to China.

SMIC has found ways to extend the capabilities of these machines through multiple pattering, which can take circuitry to 7nm, and Huawei's patented Self-Aligned Quadruple Patterning, which combines etching and lithography and might take it even to 5nm while using last-generation DUV machines.

But the following should make instantly clear that this is stretching things:

With EUV, the wavelengths of the lithography printers are 14 times smaller than DUV, at only 13.5 nm, compared to 193 nm of ArF immersion DUV systems... As an example, Intel tried to use SAQP in its first 10 nm nodes to reduce reliance on EUV, which resulted in a series of delays and complications, eventually pushing Intel into EUV.

So it's not surprising this approach has considerable disadvantages in terms of increased complexity, leading to lower yields, slower throughput, and higher production costs.

The last two solutions are the most revolutionary as they propose a different, and possibly better and cheaper way to generate EUV light.

ASML's technology is based on LPP (laser-produced plasma) and relies on high-energy lasers to bombard liquid tin droplets, creating plasma that emits EUV light. This process requires complex FPGA-based real-time control electronics to achieve precise timing and coordination.

While it has proven to be highly effective, LPP systems require sophisticated components and precise engineering, these ASML EUV systems are notoriously complex (they cost between $120M-$150M and can exceed $200M when long-term service and maintenance are factored in). Its latest high-NA EUV tool costs around $380M.

Huawei and SMEE are testing a prototype EUV system LDP (Laser-Induced Discharge Plasma) Technology as an alternative to ASML's laser-produced plasma (LPP) method. Trials are scheduled for Q3 2025, targeting mass production by 2026:

The LDP approach employed in the Chinese system generates 13.5 nm EUV radiation by vaporizing tin between electrodes and converting it to plasma via high-voltage discharge, where electron-ion collisions produce the required wavelength. This methodology offers several technical advantages over ASML's laser-produced plasma (LPP) technique, including simplified architecture, reduced footprint, improved energy efficiency, and potentially lower production costs.

It's not all plain sailing though:

this Huawei system must still answer questions about resolution capabilities, throughput stability, and integration with existing semiconductor manufacturing flows.

One should also keep in mind that LDP isn't a new solution. Early LDP systems used rotating tin supply discs as electrodes. A laser would create a pre-plasma on the tin film before a large electrical discharge generated the Z pinch and EUV light.

LDP's wheel setup offered efficiency benefits by ensuring the laser always hit tin, unlike the droplet targeting in LPP. It also provided some debris mitigation.

ASML used an LDP light source in its first Alpha demo tool but ultimately adopted LPP. The exact reasons for this shift are unclear, but a primary theory is that LDP could not scale output power as rapidly as LPP.

Extreme Technologies (which, like ASML, also comes from Philips) achieved 500W EUV power at the plasma source with LDP in 2008 but the power at intermediate focus (reaching the wafer) was significantly lower. Scaling LDP might have been hindered by the plasma becoming larger rather than brighter at a higher power.

It's not clear whether, or to what extent the Chinese efforts have overcome these power and scaling problems, if they have, they have kept that to themselves.

Asianometry concludes in a video on the subject that a domestically produced Chinese EUV machine is unlikely to be economically competitive with ASML's systems initially. It might suffer from lower wafer throughput or resolution.

That might not matter all that much to the Chinese, given the preeminent strategic importance of the technology and their ability to produce at sustained losses in order to capture learning economies.

Additionally, their LDP solution could incorporate innovative solutions, such as a different optic system to compensate for lower light source brightness.

They exhort ASML to closely monitor any developments, no doubt they are already doing just that, insofar as the Chinese produce papers about the progress they're making.

Valuation

Other SA authors have supplied their analysis, we will concentrate on a historical view:

And:

On these five valuation metrics, the shares are close to the 3-year bottom or even at the bottom. Given the growth perspective, there seems to be room for some valuation multiple expansion.

Conclusion

Several positive things are happening:

  • ASML is a premier beneficiary of the AI revolution that is still in the early innings.
  • Growth is back on track with booking booming.
  • Cash flow was particularly strong in Q4.
  • The share price has become more reasonable and is close to or at 3-year lows on multiple valuation metrics.
  • While the company can't sell its most advanced machines to China, it still generates considerable business from China selling older DUV machines.
  • However much trade relations escalate, China is unlikely to put any restrictions on ASML.
  • Its stranglehold on the top end of the market is intact, Canon's NIL machine hasn't gotten much traction and looks to be a niche solution at best, at least for now. Huawei might spin a 'DeepSeek' surprise on all of us with its LDP-based technology, but if they do, they have kept a lot of information to themselves as LDP isn't a new solution. While it has undeniable advantages, there are also important issues to be solved. And based on the arguably limited information that's out, there are no indications that Huawei has solved all of these. For the Chinese market, they don't need to, as they can sell at a subsidized loss, since ASML can't sell its EUV machines to China anyway. This isn't a disaster. Although it provides Huawei with a path to producing for learning economies, so this could eventually become a serious threat.
  • A more immediate threat is the state of the world economy (we're writing on the eve of Trump's tariff announcement). While we don't think a significant downturn in the world economy would have a big effect on AI spending, it can very well affect other chip segments relevant for ASML, so that is a risk, even though management did give a positive outlook for memory and logic, things on the ground can change quickly.

The upshot: We still think the shares are a buy here, but wouldn't go all in at this moment, given the economic uncertainties and stock market volatility.

data.title: BARK: On The Path To Reignite Growth
data.description:
A few months ago, I rated BARK, Inc. (NYSE:BARK) as a “Buy.” I liked that the business was still led by their co-founder, who was purchasing more stock in the company and I thought BARK had several ways to reignite revenue growth.

Today, I still think the business has several growth drivers which will lead to future revenue growth. Let’s dig into the latest quarter as I discuss reasons why I remain bullish on BARK.

Future Growth Drivers

BARK has numerous ways the company can grow the business to ultimately achieve profitability. While I still believe GAAP profitability is years away, it is encouraging to see BARK making strides as the company should achieve their first full year of adjusted EBITDA profitability in fiscal year 2025. On the company’s latest earnings call, BARK’s management team discussed drivers that will lead BARK to a return to growth in fiscal year 2026. These drivers include domestic & internationally expansion, streamlining operations, and growing BARK Air.

The biggest growth driver for the company has been the commerce business as revenue in that segment is up over 40%. Management still believes there is a lot of runway for domestic expansion as underdeveloped partnerships, for example those with Amazon (AMZN) and Chewy (CHWY) can likely generate further growth in the commerce segment. For Chewy specifically, the partnership started with 30 SKUs last June and now the partnership has expanded to over 150 SKUs. On the earnings call, BARK's CFO Zahir Ibrahim noted future growth with Chewy stating, “The conversations we have with Chewy are extremely positive about our performance and a ton of excitement, about the potential growth in the future. When you look at our toys performance, for example, we're already one of the biggest pet toy suppliers on their site. So just a lot of positive sentiment there.”

Similarly, BARK has expanded their partnership with Amazon as management expects this channel to grow over 70% this year. Domestically, it seems BARK has an excellent chance to continue to enhance their partnerships with two market leaders in Chewy and Amazon.

Regarding international expansion, BARK is now available on Amazon Europe and the business is partnering with Fressnapf in Europe and Pets at Home in the United Kingdom. These partners may not be as significant as BARK's domestic partners and tariffs could impact Chewy's international competitiveness but I think see this expansion as a positive.

This quarter it seemed BARK's management team placed an emphasis on the success of the business’s transition to using Shopify’s platform. BARK’s CEO Matt Meeker noted the positive results from this transition stating, “New subscriptions grew 11% year-over-year, and we achieved this at a lower customer acquisition cost. Another encouraging data point is that 43% of the checkouts on the Shopify platform last quarter were via Shop Pay. Shop Pay and Apple Pay are features that didn't exist on our legacy platforms.The new platform modernizes the customer experience, which we expect to continue to drive increased conversion over time. We plan to migrate our remaining active subscriber cohorts from our legacy sites to the Shopify platform this quarter.”

This surely seems like a benefit for the business as it hopefully makes the process easier from a consumer perspective and ultimately streamlines many aspects of BARK’s operations.

Lastly, BARK has an opportunity to expand BARK Air, which offers flights to dogs and their owners. This new business launched roughly 9 months ago and generated roughly $2 million in the quarter which is slightly more than 1% of all revenue for the quarter (noted as “Other” in the table below). The company was able to generate a positive gross profit from BARK Air. In hearing Meeker speak on the earnings call, BARK Air is a long way from generating material revenue for the business. The company plans to experiment and test out different routes and flight costs to determine demand. It does seem BARK plans to stay asset light and continue their partnerships with different aviation companies rather than purchase any planes.

I think BARK certainly has opportunities for growth ahead yet the partnerships and the growth of the commerce business will be the most important for the business. As you can see from this disaggregation of revenue table in the company’s recent 10Q filing, the company’s DTC business continues to lag QTD and YoY figures for both Toys & Accessories and Consumables.

On the earnings Meeker noted he expects the DTC business to “stabilize” in their fiscal year 2026. If the business expects to obtain overall revenue growth for the business it will come from the commerce segment and to a far less degree, BARK Air.

Financials

In Q3 2025, BARK generated revenue of roughly $126 million which is a 1.1% increase compared to Q3 2024. Direct to consumer (DTC) revenue accounted for $106 million which is a 4.3% decline compared to the prior year third quarter. Commerce revenue was the bright spot for the quarter as revenue increased by over 40% compared to the prior year quarter coming in at roughly $20 million.

Also, as you can see below gross margins have improved slightly and general and administrative expenses have declined on both a quarterly and YTD basis:

Gross margin improvement and declines general in administrative expense, coupled with a decline in interest expense have led to an improvement in net loss and net loss per common share.

BARK has a solid balance sheet with $115 million in cash and enough current assets to cover all of their current liabilities as you can see below:

The company also currently has no long-term debt which is certainly a positive.

Management feels confident in the organization’s ability to drive future growth which will lead to share price appreciation as BARK repurchased 1.7 million shares for the quarter (at an average price of $1.69 for an aggregate price of roughly $2.8 million). Since the company’s board of directors authorized the company’s buyback program in August of 2023, BARK has repurchased 11 million shares for roughly $17 million. Also, as I noted in my prior article, Meeker purchased some shares as well late in the year which I view as a bullish sign of his continued belief in the business.

Risks

Consumer spending remains a concern to some extent yet I still believe many families view pets as family members and thus are willing to spend more on their pets. Pet spending is on the rise with the average family (or individual) spending over $800 in 2023 on their pets. Although, families could certainly pull back on toy and accessory spending if necessary.

Trump’s tariffs could still impact the overall business as well as the tariff game continues to play out. Regarding tariffs here is what Meeker had to say, “One-third of our volumes come from consumables, which are almost entirely sourced domestically and are unaffected by the latest tariffs. For the remaining two-third, we have been proactively working with our key suppliers in China to implement productivity improvements that dampen the impact of the latest 10% tariffs. And as one of the largest dog toy companies in the U.S., our scale, coupled with our strong gross margins, provide flexibility to address these costs.”

BARK may even benefit to some extent as “cheaper” dog toys may have a more difficult time competing in the United States if international competitors have to raise costs. For American consumers, if cost isn’t a factor I think BARK would have an advantage in toy quality and uniqueness.

Valuation

BARK has a valuation grade of a "C-" at Seeking Alpha:

As Bark is still unprofitable on a GAAP basis, I believe price to sales is likely the best metric for this organization. Currently, Bark has a price to sales ratio (forward) of 0.49 compared to the sector median of 0.82. On a TTM basis the company's current ratio of 0.50 is also below the sector median and is trading near the company's five year average. As you can see below this PS ratio has come down over the past few months as the overall market entered into correction territory:

Given this market correction, and BARK's historical averages I would say their current valuation seems reasonable and is more favorable compared to a few months ago.

Analysts seem to believe BARK can grow revenue in the high single to low double-digits over the next few years and Wall St. is very bullish on the business as analysts state the company is a "Strong Buy" with tremendous upside as you can see below:

Conclusion

I think they are enough positive catalysts to say that even if BARK Air doesn’t generate more revenue in fiscal year 2026 and even if international revenue doesn’t grow substantially, the company still has enough white space domestically and has at least created more operational efficiencies which will result in improvements to the business’s bottom line.

BARK's s partnerships will continue to expand in the months and likely years to come and this will also result in more brand awareness. Despite BARK being subject to consumer spending patterns, families will continue to spend money on their dogs which will bode well for BARK.

Although this is a risky, volatile stock I believe BARK is on the right path to reignite revenue growth for the business. Investors with an appetite for risk should take advantage of the recent market correction and consider adding some shares of this business on the rebound.

data.title: IAC: Paving A Path For Value To Be Unlocked
data.description:
Investment thesis

I see a clear path for value to be unlocked in shares of IAC (NASDAQ:IAC) following the Angi (ANGI) spin-off. I recently covered Angi with a Hold rating, given its declining revenue and stagnant earnings. With it now being an independent company, IAC's remaining holdings consist of high-quality businesses, which for the most part have attractive growth outlooks. While my estimate for intrinsic value shows an upside of between 20% to 50% based on the sum of the parts, I importantly identify two key catalysts related to the aggressive share buybacks in Q1 as well as lower corporate costs going forward, which should narrow the current conglomerate discount which IAC shares face today. I have adopted a conservative approach towards valuation, which limits downside risk, thereby setting up an attractive risk-reward opportunity for investors to go Long IAC.

Valuing its core holdings

IAC's largest asset is its stake in MGM Resorts (MGM), which corresponds to 64.7 million shares, currently worth $1.94 billion. Since MGM has been covered elaborately on this platform with positive ratings, I will not dwell too much into why I think it's worth more than its current market value. While the stock currently trades very cheaply relative to the quality of the business, at just a mid-single digit multiple of FCF, I will share the commentary given by IAC's Chairman during the Q4 earnings call on MGM, which I completely agree with. He stated:

I think MGM is in excellent shape, excellent operating results and a superb management team. I consider it to be a forever asset.

The second-largest holding is Dotdash Meredith ("DDM"), which is wholly owned and operated by IAC. I value this business at $1.3 billion or an enterprise value of $2.6 billion, when accounting for its net debt of $1.3 billion. This translates to an EV/Adjusted EBITDA multiple that is less than 8, based on the lower end of management's guided Adjusted EBITDA range of $330 million to $350 million. Taking my valuation a step further, I see the business trading at a Price/FCF multiple that is less than 10. I arrive at my estimate for FCF of approximately $131 million by deducting interest expenses of $85 million, capex of $75 million, and tax payments of $40 million from the Adjusted EBITDA figure.

I believe this valuation is very reasonable for a high-quality content publisher with premium inventory, which is forecasted to grow at high single-digit rates in the medium term, with incremental margins that are greater than 40%. While site traffic and ad prices can fluctuate, the increased adoption of its D/Cipher targeting tool across its sites should continue to lift overall CPMs. While relevant public market peers are hard to find, it is noteworthy that content publishers like the New York Times (NYT), with similar growth rates, trade at Price/FCF multiples that are close to 20, allowing for a large premium since NYT's subscription revenue is much more stable when compared to DDM's ad-based revenue.

Other holdings and sum of the parts valuation

Turning to IAC's other significant holdings, we have its 30% stake in Turo. While the company recently scrapped its plan for an IPO, its financials for the first nine months of 2024 are publicly available. It posted year-over-year revenue growth of 10% for the first nine months of 2024, with a corresponding net income of $19.3 million.

When it comes to valuing Turo, I adopt a relative valuation, considering Airbnb (ABNB) as a relevant public market peer having similar growth rates. With ABNB stock trading at 8 times gross profit, I instead take Turo's contribution profit as it is a more comparable metric, as shown above. I then apply the same multiple, but with a 20% discount in order to account for Turo's weaker profitability and smaller scale. Based on an estimated value of $210 million in contribution profit for FY24, I arrive at an equity value of $1.34 billion, thus valuing IAC's stake at $400 million. Notably, this is only slightly higher than the $1.2 billion valuation Turo garnered in 2020.

Turning to Care.com, the business outlook is less promising with revenue expected to remain stagnant, with modest earnings growth. While this is a capital-light business that benefits from network effects, I think it is prudent to value it at $500 million, which implies a multiple of 10 based on the midpoint of its FY25 Adjusted EBITDA guidance of $50 million. This is also the value that IAC paid to acquire it in early 2020.

As shown in the table above, I arrive at a sum of the parts valuation of $4.94 billion, or $61.8 per share. This represents an upside of over 50% from the current share price of around $40. When considering the net cash position, I have deducted $300 million from the $1.1 billion that was available when exiting Q4, to account for the share buybacks which have been conducted during Q1.

This is evident when we look at the shares outstanding figure presented along with the company's SEC filing on the 28th of March. This points to a 6 million or 7% reduction in shares outstanding in Q1 alone, with shares outstanding now at 80 million compared to 86 million at the end of Q4. This is a substantial increase in the amount of capital being returned to shareholders, given that the company had not done any buybacks over the past two years. This is an important catalyst that should help shares re-rate higher and close the gap between my estimate for intrinsic value and the current share price. The magnitude of the buybacks also emphasizes management's view of how undervalued shares are. Given the large net cash position as well as the strong FCF generated from its operating businesses, DDM and Care.com, there is ample room for buybacks to continue at this pace in the coming quarters.

Now, an argument against my valuation model from many in the investment community is that it doesn't account for the negative impact from corporate costs. With corporate costs estimated to amount to $140 million in FY25 (including $50 million in one-time expenses related to the Angi spin-off), this is indeed an aspect that negatively impacts IAC's valuation, and likely causes it to trade at a large discount. Nevertheless, a second catalyst I expect going forward is for these costs to significantly come down due to the restructuring done in Q4 as well as the shifting of certain expenses over to Angi, as part of the spin-off process. Therefore, I expect corporate costs to be well below $90 million on an annual basis going forward, which should substantially improve IAC's bottom-line performance. In order to be conservative, one could deduct an equity value of $900 million, implying 10 times multiples on annualized corporate expenses from my valuation model, and still arrive at an estimate for intrinsic value which is above $50 per share, implying 20% upside.

I will point out to readers that I have intentionally left out IAC's search and emerging businesses (including Vivian Health) from my valuation model. They currently do not contribute meaningfully to earnings, and I do not find them to be relevant to my investment thesis.

Risks to consider

Capital allocation by management remains a key risk that investors should be aware of and track closely. Leadership has recently changed with Barry Diller taking over from Joey Levin, and he has subsequently focused solely on buybacks, which are very accretive at these levels. However, I would point out that he, as Chairman, had previously been interested in acquisitions of media companies like Paramount Global, a move I am glad as a shareholder they did not proceed with. While management has generated immense shareholder value over the last two decades, their recent track record pertaining to the acquisitions of Care.com and Meredith has fallen well short of expectations.

Besides this, a weaker macroeconomic environment will no doubt negatively impact all of IAC's business, with DDM likely more impacted given its high dependency on advertising revenue.

IAC stock is a Buy

I believe that the spin-off of ANGI has brought about a couple of key catalysts which should lift IAC stock higher and closer to my estimate for intrinsic value. I see an upside of between 20% to 50%, with limited downside given the conservativeness in my estimates and low valuation multiples applied. I am therefore rating IAC stock as a Buy.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best Stock With A Catalyst investment competition, which runs through April 17. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

data.title: CoreWeave: AI Darling With A Debt Problem
data.description:
Investment Thesis

CoreWeave (NASDAQ:CRWV) IPOs with approximately 469,724,446 shares if the underwriters exercise their overallotment option in full (page 17). This means that its market cap is around $24 billion, give or take.

The issue here is that the business is badly hemorrhaging cash. I suspect that this year, at its present run-rate, CoreWeave is going to burn through about $5 billion of free cash flow.

Now, the thesis gets further complicated, since the market is very much disenchanted with AI hyperscalers. That being said, I believe that together with the funds raised from its IPO, plus committed financing, CoreWeave will continue to exist and will plow forward.

However, successfully raising capital is one endeavor. Running a profitable business is quite another.

And if this wasn't enough drama, retail investors appeared to have ignited a meme-like attitude towards CoreWeave.

With my popcorn ready, I watch this story unfold from the sidelines.

CoreWeave's Near-Term Prospects

CoreWeave provides cloud infrastructure specifically designed for AI. Unlike traditional cloud platforms built for general computing tasks, CoreWeave focuses on high-performance computing to power AI applications.

Its value proposition is that it offers specialized hardware that makes AI models train faster and scale more effectively.

This helps AI companies and researchers save time and costs while achieving better performance.

In a nutshell, CoreWeave's infrastructure is used by major AI firms like OpenAI, Microsoft (MSFT), and NVIDIA (NVDA), making it a key player in the AI revolution.

With this context in mind, let's now discuss its fundamentals.

This is a Growth Story, But a Sustainability Question Looms Large

CoreWeave is a growth story. As such, the fact that it reported more than 700% y/y revenue growth rates should come as no surprise. Nobody is questioning that aspect of the story, that is the bull case in all its splendor, after all.

The questions start immediately after that.

Without overcomplicating matters, the main question in my mind is what sort of growth rate can we earnestly expect in the next twelve months?

Is it the case that over the next twelve months, CoreWeave is still growing at more than 500% y/y? Or could it be the case that this company's growth rates will be around 100% y/y?

You may contend, what difference does it make? Growth is growth! The issue is that the sustainability of its growth rates will have a dramatic impact on the right sort of valuation, it makes sense to get involved with CRWV.

CRWV Stock Valuation -- 5x Forward Sales

As an Inflection investor, I like to back companies with a solid 5% of their market caps made up of cash. It's not that I'm old-fashioned. But I know that in the markets, there are old investors. And there are bold investors. There are no old and bold investors.

Along these lines, note that CoreWeave ended Q4 2024 with approximately $5.2 billion of net debt, if we include the $1.5 billion raised through its recent IPO. This means that investors are paying around a $24 billion market cap for a business that holds about 20% of its market cap as net debt. That's not an attractive risk-reward.

On top of that, CoreWeave has about $2.5 billion of debt that has been made current. This means that CoreWeave is going to have either pay off or refinance this debt in the coming twelve months. Since the business holds just $2.8 billion of cash, including its raised funds from its IPO, this means that this business is not on a steady footing. Quite on the contrary.

Furthermore, CoreWeave is burning through approximately $5 billion of free cash flow per year. This figure is naturally not sustainable, given that CoreWeave will be close to running out of cash, so CoreWeave will have to raise more funds quickly.

I suspect that CoreWeave will not need to aggressively dilute investors, since it has at least $4 billion of off-balance sheet available credit facilities. But as noted earlier, raising cash is one aspect of a successful business. But running a profitable enterprise that produces clean free cash flow is quite another.

Yet another problem is that more than 60% of the business is tied to Microsoft (MSFT). And Microsoft isn't a charity. This means that all the negotiation power lies at Microsoft's feet.

Will Microsoft continue to heavily invest even more in its AI hyperscaler needs? Or will Microsoft take a period of time to digest some of its heavy investments in 2024?

Basically, what sort of guidance should we expect to see? Can we expect around a 100% y/y increase in revenues? Perhaps more? One way or another, this business is probably priced at more than 5x forward sales, which the bulls could argue is attractive, for its growth rates.

Potential Risk Factors to the Upside

Investors latch onto an idea, and that idea can rapidly turn into a bubble.

Even if I'm bearish on CoreWeave as I can't make much headway in grasping simple questions such as its path to positive free cash flows, or even, more elementary questions, such as its revenue growth rates for 2025, in the short term, the market doesn't appear to care much about this.

The stock could easily double and move to $100 per share in the coming weeks, leaving the stock priced at around 10x forward sales.

Another upside risk factor is that a different hyperscaler business could become CoreWeave's customer, which would diversify its customer concentration risk factor and remove one significant overhang from its stock.

The Bottom Line

For me, CoreWeave is a clear pass. The company is burning cash at an unsustainable rate, carrying significant debt, and faces heavy dependence on Microsoft, which holds all the leverage.

While its revenue growth is impressive, the real question is sustainability—and I don’t see a clear path to profitability.

Sure, the stock might rally on hype, but I'm not interested in betting on a meme-like frenzy. I’ll be watching from the sidelines with my popcorn, but my money is staying far away.

data.title: Westport Fuel Systems: Burning The Furniture To Heat The House - Sell
data.description:
Note:

I have covered Westport Fuel Systems Inc. or "Westport" (NASDAQ:WPRT) previously, so investors should view this as an update to my earlier articles on the company.

Westport Fuel Systems Overview

On Monday, Westport Fuel Systems reported Q4 and full-year 2024 results, with revenues coming in ahead of expectations and surprisingly strong cash generation:

The company generated a whopping $22.6 million in free cash flow in the fourth quarter and ended the year with $37.6 million in cash and cash equivalents, as well as $33.7 million in debt.

However, increased cash generation was mainly a result of lower working capital requirements and proceeds received from the transfer of Westport's High Pressure Direct Injection ("HPDI") operations to a recently established joint venture ("Cespira") with key customer AB Volvo or "Volvo" (OTCPK:VLVLY, OTCPK:VOLAF, OTCPK:VOLVF).

Subsequent to year-end, the company collected an $11.4 million holdback receivable from Cummins Inc. (CMI) which covered extended warranty obligations related to the dissolved Cummins Westport joint venture.

Unfortunately, management does not expect the recent improvement to continue, as evidenced by the company's surprise going concern warning:

Based on our projected capital expenditures, debt servicing obligations and operating requirements under our current business plan, we are projecting that our cash and cash equivalents will not be sufficient to fund our operations through the next twelve months from the date of the issuance of this MD&A.

These conditions raise substantial doubt about Westport's ability continue as a going concern within one year after the date our December 31, 2024 Consolidated Financial Statements are issued.

To alleviate the concern, Westport has agreed to divest its core Light-Duty operations, which represented 87% of the company's sales last year:

Please note that the Light-Duty business was Westport's sole profitable segment. Going forward, the company will focus on its remaining Heavy-Duty OEM and High Pressure Controls & Systems operations:

This decision is expected to enable us to concentrate fully on providing affordable solutions for hard to decarbonize mobility applications like long haul and heavy-duty trucking that can take advantage of the unique, practical and affordable HPDI technology and our world class high-pressure components and systems technologies and scalable alternative fuel solutions, ensuring that we remain at the forefront of emissions-reducing innovations that are cost effective.

However, with Cespira being an unconsolidated entity and the High Pressure Controls & Systems segment representing less than 3% of total revenues in 2024, the remaining Westport Fuel Systems will be a tiny, unprofitable stub. Please note also that the company's transitional services agreement with Cespira is scheduled to expire at the end of Q2/2026 which will effectively reduce Heavy-Duty OEM segment revenues to zero.

Moreover, Cespira will likely require additional capital contributions over time.

While the buyer of the Light-Duty operations will assume approximately $27 million in related debt, the purchase price also includes the segment's (undisclosed) cash balances.

Assuming $20 million of cash being part of the Light-Duty segment, Westport's post-transaction cash balance would calculate to approximately $100 million. According to statements made by management on the conference call, the company would retain approximately $7 million in debt outstanding under its credit facility with Export Development Canada.

However, with the remaining business likely to generate substantially negative free cash flow for the foreseeable future, market participants are likely to assign a substantially negative enterprise value going forward.

Fellow Canadian alternative energy play Ballard Power Systems (BLDP) is experiencing the very same issue, as the company is currently trading at a more than 40% discount to its estimated cash balance at the end of Q1.

Depending on the company's cash usage going forward, Westport's runway might have been extended by several years:

However, cash burn could increase if management decides to pursue inorganic growth.

With large parts of the company having been sold in recent quarters, and prospects for the remaining operations being muted at best, I expect shares to mark new multi-year lows sooner rather than later.

Consequently, I am downgrading Westport Fuel Systems' common shares from "Hold" to "Sell".

Risk Factors

Quite frankly, I am struggling to come up with credible near-term catalysts for the stock other than a surprise move into one of the market's recent hype themes (Space, AI, Defense, Energy Storage, Nuclear, Crypto) but in the current environment, market participants are not likely to blindly chase shares of the latest free rider company.

Bottom Line

While Westport Fuel Systems reported reasonably good fourth quarter results, the company's going concern warning caught investors flat-footed.

To alleviate the concern, Westport has agreed to sell its core Light-Duty operations, which contributed the vast majority of the company's sales in recent years.

While net proceeds could help Westport Fuel Systems staying afloat well into the next decade, uncertainties regarding the company's ability to essentially start from scratch are likely to result in market participants assigning a substantially negative enterprise value to the remaining business for the time being.

With shares likely to mark new multi-year lows in the near future, I am downgrading Westport Fuel Systems' common shares from "Hold" to "Sell".

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Billionaire Investor Warns Of 'Significant Correction' And Buys High Yield: Our Picks
data.description:
Steve Cohen - Billionaire investor, New York Mets baseball team owner, and founder of Point72 Asset Management hedge fund - recently discussed his increasing bearishness on both the broader economy and the stock market itself. Additionally, he recently signaled his bullishness on the private credit space and, as a result, launched a new investment strategy at his hedge fund into the high-yield lending space. In this article, we will discuss further why Mr. Cohen is bearish on the economy and market, yet bullish on private credit, and then share some of our top picks in the high-yield private credit space right now in light of the recently souring economic outlook.

Why Steven Cohen Is Bearish On The Market & Bullish On High Yield

The reason Mr. Cohen is bearish on the market and the broader economy in general right now is due primarily to the impact of tariffs, slowing immigration, and government spending cuts. As he stated in a recent interview,

Tariffs cannot be positive, okay? I mean, it’s a tax. On top of that, we have slowing immigration, which means the labor force will not grow as rapidly as it has over the last five years.

Meanwhile, in reference to the effect of government spending cuts, he said,

When that money has been coursing through the economy over many years, now potential will be reduced or stopped in many ways. It’s got to be negative for the economy.

As a result, he thinks that the U.S. economy will likely slow its growth in the second half of the year, which could lead to a material pullback in the stock market. He stated,

My view [on the U.S. economy and market] is negative for the first time in a while, and I think we’re seeing the regime shift a little bit. It may only last a year or so, but it’s definitely a period where I think the best gains have been had. It wouldn’t surprise me to see a significant correction."

At the same time, he said, "I don’t think it’s going to be a disaster."

What this all points to is a period of likely stagflation, in which inflation remains higher for longer and growth slows meaningfully, weakening stocks that are susceptible to inflation and or slowing growth. This is particularly relevant given that the market appears to remain expensive despite the recent pullback in the S&P 500 (SPY) and Nasdaq (QQQ) to begin 2025, as numerous valuation models indicate.

This, in turn, helps to explain some of the bullishness that Stephen Cohen has on private credit, in addition to the fact that he believes demand significantly exceeds supply in the private credit space.

The reason why private credit is not a bad place to be investing right now—and which likely explains, at least in part, why Mr. Cohen has recently launched a new investment fund in the space at his hedge fund—is because private credit typically enjoys senior secured status in the companies to which the loans are made. This provides contractual returns that have a pretty good chance of achieving considerable recovery of the principal even if the borrower defaults. Additionally, these loans are typically small enough in nature that it is not too difficult to build a well-diversified portfolio of them, which further reduces the risks associated with the strategy. Moreover, the returns are quite high, especially when a prudent amount of leverage is applied, leading to double-digit returns on equity in most cases.

On top of that, the loans in private credit are often indexed to short-term interest rates and float off of them, thereby making them fairly inflation-resistant products. Given that higher inflation tends to produce higher interest rates, in an environment where inflation is elevated, these loans tend to achieve higher net interest spreads. Therefore, private credit can provide an attractive risk-adjusted return in a stagflationary environment, since the returns on equity are similar to those of common equity while enjoying greater defensiveness due to broad diversification and the senior secured status of the loans—along with a yield on those loans that actually increases with interest rates. This contrasts with many other products that pay high yields, such as REITs (VNQ), utilities (XLU), and infrastructure businesses (UTF), which tend to generate lower returns when interest rates rise.

Our Top Picks

With this outlook for the economy, market, and private credit space in view, how are we approaching our portfolio? Like Mr. Cohen, we think that the market is currently significantly overvalued and likely due for underperformance in the near term, especially if there is additional economic turbulence caused by Trump’s tariffs, even though we do think that government spending cuts are a net positive for the economy over the long term. At the same time, we also think that private credit offers an attractive risk-adjusted place to allocate capital right now. That being said, many publicly traded private credit companies such as BDCs (BIZD) like Ares Capital (ARCC), Main Street Capital (MAIN), and Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL) trade at premium valuations right now. Therefore, we do not think that they present compelling value.

However, there are some better opportunities right now, like Kayne Anderson BDC (KBDC) and Morgan Stanley Direct Lending (MSDL), which have virtually their entire portfolios invested in senior secured loans, are well diversified, and have prudently structured balance sheets. This makes them fairly defensive plays in the current environment, while at the same time trading roughly in line or even at a slight discount to their net asset values. As a result, they present much more attractive value for investors than some of the bigger names in this space. As a result, we are focusing our portfolio exclusively on these opportunities instead.

Investor Takeaway

As this article points out, the U.S. economy is facing uncertainty as the Trump administration’s push for massive new tariffs is likely going to put a strain on the economy—at least in the near term. Just how significantly it will impact the economy has yet to be determined, but when combining the potential headwinds from tariffs—which even other major investors like BlackRock’s (BLK) CEO Larry Fink have said are leading to a slowdown in the economy—with short-term headwinds from government spending cuts and potential near-term economic disruptions from reduced illegal immigration, there is a very distinct possibility of a near-term economic slowdown or even a recession, along with an accompanying market pullback from its current richly valued state.

Over the long term, we have a more positive view of the economic impacts of some of these changes, but in the meantime, we are trying to become increasingly cautious and prudent in our portfolio disposition. Part of this is increasing our exposure to private credit instead of some of the more aggressive equity investment opportunities out there. However, even then, we are still being very careful and only investing in BDCs with portfolios that are almost entirely first-lien senior secured and that have strong underwriting, conservative balance sheets, and valuations that are at or below net asset value. For example, we recently purchased shares in several conservatively positioned BDCs - including KBDC in our Retirement Portfolio - at High Yield Investor.

data.title: SMA Solar Technology: Still A Buy, But More Conservative Outlook
data.description:
Since my last article on SMA Solar Technology (OTCPK:SMTGF)(OTCPK:SMTGY), although only a few months have passed, it feels like years from a geopolitical perspective. The company faces new challenges, such as the potential imposition of import tariffs in the US, but also new opportunities, such as Germany's expansive fiscal policy.

In this context, the inverter market, despite starting to show tepid signs of improvement, is still characterized by oversupply. Companies are struggling to sell off the large amount of products in inventory, leading to significant write-offs. SMA itself, in FY24, recorded an inventory impairment of €113 million, representing 7.4% of revenue.

Considering SMA strong position in the utility-scale inverter market and management's efforts to implement operating cost cuts, I confirm the "buy" rating for the company. However, the intensification of geopolitical risks and the persistence of oversupply in the inverter market signal rising uncertainty about its activities. For this reason, the new target price, determined by the DCF analysis, drops from €23.75 per share in October 2024 to €20.51.

Business Update

SMA's financial results in FY24 were extremely dependent on the Large Scale & Project Solutions segment, which posted 38.9% YoY revenue growth, accounting for 76.8% of total sales compared to 44.3% in FY23. The high utility-scale PV system demand in both Europe and the US, resulting from the steady decline in LCOE, boosted the growth of the business unit, positioning SMA as one of the leading players in string and centralized inverters. This trend is also reflected in the segment's EBIT margin, which grew from 12.28% in FY23 to 19.34% in FY24. This increase, although partly driven by greater scalability due to increased production, also indicates a significant imbalance between supply and demand for these products, allowing SMA to raise their selling prices.

A completely opposite picture emerges for the Home Solutions and C&I Solutions segments, which experienced a general slump in both revenue and margins. In FY24, the two business units experienced a 70.7% and 61.6% collapse in sales, respectively, resulting in EBIT margins deep in the red. The latter result was also hit by a significant inventory impairment in Q4 FY24, amounting to €44.6 million (plus €4.2 million in production line impairment) for the Home Solutions segment and €49.5 million for the C&I Solutions segment.

I believe that the main reason for this outcome lies in the market's preference for microinverters designed for distributed PV systems. Indeed, this product has certain characteristics that make it more suitable for installation on residential or industrial PV systems. They have a lower cost and are significantly smaller, which is essential given the limited area of a rooftop.

Furthermore, the IRA prioritized microinverters by providing an Advanced Manufacturing Tax Credit (45X) of up to $0.11/W, despite their lower efficiency compared to other types of inverters. This impact is evident when looking at the evolution of Enphase Energy's (ENPH) (specialized in microinverters) EBIT margin compared to SMA and SolarEdge (SEDG). Unlike its two competitors, Enphase recovered much faster from the oversupply condition and closed the second half of 2024 with a positive and growing EBIT margin.

One last consideration that might disadvantage SMA compared to Enphase concerns the location of its production sites. The tax credits introduced by the IRA favor and better compensate components produced within the US. For this reason, SMA, having limited manufacturing capacity in the US, faces a competitive disadvantage compared to domestic companies.

Restructuring Program

In response to the challenging market situation, especially in the Home Solutions and C&I Solutions segments, SMA's management has planned a corporate restructuring that aims for an EBIT improvement of €200 million by FY27, with an expected positive impact of approximately €80 million by FY25. To reach this goal, Olaf Heyden has been appointed as Chief Transformation and Operational Officer, temporarily until FY26. The plan includes an organization-wide restructuring, involving the merger of the two business units mentioned above, as well as various measures designed to increase operational efficiency:

Optimizing prices and sales volumes to increase revenue. Of all the planned measures, this appears to be the most speculative, with no clear disclosure of the actual strategy for its implementation.

Optimization of material costs through a renegotiation process with suppliers, including the search for cheaper alternatives.

Optimization of operating costs by reducing fixed expenses to provide more flexibility in the event of adverse economic conditions for the company. A lower incidence of fixed costs on total costs makes it easier to implement cuts when necessary. In detail, a reduction in indirect costs related to fleet, IT licenses, and office supplies is planned.

Staff downsizing. Optimizing the organizational structure by merging the Home Solutions and C&I Solutions segments, while simultaneously limiting new hires. Both measures are directed at reducing administrative costs.

Tailwind From German Fiscal Stimulus

In March 2025, Germany announced a massive €1 trillion fiscal stimulus package aimed at reviving growth after several years marked by a steady mild contraction of the economy. Behind this landmark decision, which includes a loosening of the constitutional debt brake, lies the desire to limit the factors that have adversely affected the German economy since pandemic, foremost among them the high cost of energy. For this reason, although a considerable part of the stimulus will be allocated to military spending as a consequence of Russia's aggressive military policy, a large share of the package will be directed toward infrastructure, ecological transition, and the modernization of productive capital.

Given the limited energy resource capacity in the country, solar, wind, nuclear, and hydrogen energy will play a key role in achieving greater energy independence. As SMA is one of Germany's largest companies in the sector, I believe it will be able to capitalize on funds aimed at improving the company's production capacity and efficiency. Additionally, these funds are expected to lead to increased demand for utility-scale PV systems, both in Germany and Europe, with a positive effect on inverter demand. For this reason, I consider this development a significant opportunity for SMA in the medium to long-term horizon.

Financial Update

In FY24, SMA reported revenue down 19.6% YoY, attributable to a 4.5% reduction in inverter output sold (19.5 GW vs 20.4 GW in FY23) and a drop in selling prices. The oversupply condition led to an inventory impairment of €113 million plus a €33 million provision for severance. These one-off costs negatively impacted margins and profitability, pushing EBIT and net income into deep negative territory.

Considering an inventory of €563 million, I believe it is likely that the company will incur additional asset impairments in FY25 as well. For this reason, I lowered the revenue and margin estimates for FY25 and FY26 compared to my previous article. Management released guidance projecting revenue in the range of €1.5 billion to €1.65 billion and a midpoint EBITDA of €90 million for FY25. The effects of the restructuring are expected to drive margins improvement through FY27.

The net cash position of €84 million as of December 2024, though sharply down from €283 million as of December 2023, should provide the company with enough cash to fund the €115 million in CAPEX planned for FY25. In addition, the company is likely to generate a positive OCF in FY25, aided by higher EBITDA and a reduction in NWC, which was €473 million as of December 2024.

Main Risks

The imposition of tariffs on electrical components or generalized tariffs on the EU would have a major negative impact on SMA's bottom line. The German company generated 40% of its FY24 revenue in the Americas segment. The region is also the largest contributor to the order backlog as of December 2024 with a 49% share.

The inverter market is characterized by high competition from Chinese companies and the increasing competitiveness of US companies as a consequence of the IRA. Although this may boost demand for utility-scale PV systems in the US, it also enhances the competitiveness of US inverter manufacturers, consequently weakening SMA's position, especially in the Home Solutions and C&I Solutions segments.

The ongoing oversupply in the inverter market, primarily in Europe, could lead to weak results for SMA in FY25 as well.

Inflation and high interest rates negatively affect demand for new utility-scale PV systems, hindering SMA's revenue growth.

Discounted Cash Flow

To attribute a valuation to SMA Solar Technology, I performed a three-stage DCF. I made an estimate of the expected values of revenue, EBIT margin, CAPEX and depreciation for FY25, FY26, FY27. I made a projection of these values for the period between FY28 and FY34, finally calculating the terminal value. This analysis returned a target price of €20.51 per share, which is about 35% higher than SMA current market price. To determine this value, I made the following assumptions:

Beta was obtained using the European unlevered beta of "Electrical Equipment" industry reported by Professor Aswath Damodaran, subsequently calculating levered beta based on SMA D/E ratio. A value of 1.17 was derived.

Risk-free rate of 3.39%, based on average of the 10-year government bonds of the countries where SMA operates.

Equity risk premium of 4.83% was obtained from the weighted average of the ERPs of the countries where SMA operates. Countries' ERPs are provided by Aswath Damodaran. Cost of equity of 9.05% derived from CAPM model.

WACC of 9.05%.

Growth rate (g) of 2%.

Takeaway

SMA Solar Technology holds a strong competitive position in the utility-scale PV system inverter market. In addition, the German fiscal stimulus could provide an expansion opportunity for the company, supporting higher revenue growth. On the other hand, the restructuring undertaken by management in FY24 will optimize operating costs, positively affecting margins as early as FY25. Despite these strengths, the business in which the company operates is exposed to several risks, such as tariffs and the loss of competitiveness in US of the Home Solutions and C&I Solutions segments due to the IRA. Considering the DCF result, which indicates a potential upside of more than 35%, I assign SMA a "buy" rating, despite lowering the target price from €23.75 per share (October 2024 article) to €20.51.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Chart Of The Day: It's Tariff Day! Time To 'Buy The News?'
data.description:
By Mike Larson

“Buy the rumor, sell the news.” It’s an old Wall Street saying you’ve probably heard a million times.

But now that it’s Tariff Day, should you do the opposite? Is it time to buy the news?

Think about it: We’ve been selling the rumor of higher tariffs and a worsening trade war for weeks. Ever since the markets peaked in February, the averages got smacked hard enough to push the S&P 500 into an “official” correction - down 10% from its peak. Many of the highest-flying Artificial Intelligence (AI) and related stocks fared even worse. Even the Roundhill Magnificent Seven ETF (MAGS) is now down around 14% on the year.

But look at the MoneyShow Chart of the Day here. It shows the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY). We had a high-volume selloff in February and March. Then we had a re-test on Monday... and it was successful (for now at least). Specifically, we tagged the previous low, then mounted a high-volume reversal to the upside -- and recorded a slightly higher low in RSI.

SPY: Is This the Start of a Bottoming Process?

Data by YCharts

I recently wrote about what a bottoming process might look like. And you know what? The action over the last few days looks kind of like that.

It’s only a start. Maybe President Trump’s tariff plans will be so aggressive, the short-term technical action won’t matter and we’ll just give up the ghost. But maybe... just maybe... the best play is to buy the news here!

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
